title: Kundalini and Death: How Awakening Changes Your Relationship With Mortality

body:
Kundalini and Death: How Awakening Changes Your Relationship With Mortality

You're probably reading this because you're scared. You're feeling things you can't explain, things that are tearing your old reality to shreds, and maybe, just maybe, you're wondering if you're dying. Or if you should be dying. Right? I've been there. I know that feeling of existential dread that creeps in at 3 AM, when the world is quiet and your own mind is screaming. It's a special kind of hell, and it's often part and parcel with a kundalini awakening. This isn't some fluffy spiritual talk; this is real, raw, and it can feel like you're staring death in the face every single day.

When kundalini kicks off, it doesn't just rearrange your chakras or give you pretty visions. It detonates your entire sense of self, and with that, your relationship with the ultimate unknown: death. You see, before, death was probably an abstract concept, something that happened to other people, way off in the future. Now? Now it feels like it's breathing down your neck. You're not alone in that. It's a common, terrifying side effect of this process. I'll tell you what, it's not just a fear of dying; it's a fear of what you're becoming, or what you're losing. It's the ego, that little self you've built your whole life around, finally seeing its expiration date.

Look, I'm not going to sugarcoat this for you. Kundalini can feel like a death. It's often called "ego death" for a reason. Your old self, your old beliefs, your old ways of seeing the world - they're all being incinerated. And that's terrifying. It feels like you're losing your mind, losing control, losing everything you thought defined you. But here's the thing: you're not dying in the conventional sense. You're being reborn. And rebirth, by definition, requires a death of what was before. It's messy, it's painful, and it's utterly disorienting. You're in the crucible, my friend, and it's burning away everything that isn't truly you.

The Ego's Last Stand

So, what's actually happening when you feel like you're dying? It's your ego, your constructed identity, fighting for its life. It's a powerful force, that ego. It's kept you safe, given you a sense of belonging, and told you who you are for decades. Now, kundalini is coming through and saying, "Nope, that's not the whole story. That's not even half the story." And the ego rebels. It throws everything it has at you: fear, anxiety, depression, a sense of impending doom. It tells you you're going crazy, that you're sick, that you're going to die alone and afraid. It's trying to get you to stop, to retreat, to go back to the comfortable illusion of your old self.

I remember lying in bed, drenched in sweat, heart pounding, convinced I was having a heart attack or a stroke. Every little twitch, every strange sensation, was a sign of my imminent demise. I'd call friends, desperate for reassurance, only to feel even more isolated because they couldn't possibly understand what I was going through. The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) really helped me understand that these physical sensations, while terrifying, weren't necessarily signs of physical illness, but often manifestations of the energy moving through my system. It's a classic kundalini symptom, this intense fear of death. It's the ego's way of trying to maintain control. It's saying, "If I can't be in charge, then let's just end it all!" It's dramatic, you know? But it feels very, very real.

You'll find yourself questioning everything. Your career, your relationships, your past, your future. All of it. It's like a cosmic audit, and nothing is spared. This can feel like a death of your old life, your old dreams, your old aspirations. And in a way, it is. But it's making room for something new, something more authentic, something that's actually aligned with your true self, not just the self you thought you were supposed to be. It's a brutal process, but it's necessary. You can't build a new house without tearing down the old, dilapidated one first. And right now, you're in the demolition phase.

The Illusion of Separation

One of the biggest things kundalini does is shatter the illusion of separation. Before, you probably felt like a distinct, separate individual, completely cut off from everything else. You had your body, your mind, your thoughts, and that was "you." But as kundalini activates, you start to feel connected. Sometimes it's a subtle hum, sometimes it's an overwhelming sense of oneness that can be both beautiful and utterly terrifying. This connection extends beyond other people, beyond nature; it extends to the very fabric of existence. And that means it extends to what you call "death."

When you start to experience yourself as part of something much larger, the idea of your individual self ceasing to exist becomes less frightening. It doesn't mean the fear completely vanishes, because you're still in a human body with human instincts. But the terror starts to loosen its grip. You begin to understand that death isn't an ending, but a transformation, a return to the source. It's not a void; it's a dissolution into something greater. I'm not saying you'll suddenly be skipping through graveyards, whistling a happy tune. But the gnawing dread, the cold sweat of existential terror, it starts to recede. You begin to grasp, not just intellectually but experientially, that you are not just this body, this mind. You are something more. Something eternal.

This is where things get really weird for most people. You might have experiences where you feel like you're leaving your body, or you're seeing things from a different perspective. These aren't hallucinations, not in the traditional sense. They're glimpses behind the veil, cracks in the illusion of your separate self. And they can be incredibly unsettling. You might think you're having a psychotic break, and honestly, it can feel a lot like one. That's why it's so important to have some kind of guidance, even if it's just a book that tells you what's going on. When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) is a good one for this, because it talks about how disorienting this process can be.

The awareness that you are more than your physical form can bring a strange peace. It's not a peace that erases all fear, but a peace that puts fear in perspective. You realize that even if this body dies, you don't. The consciousness, the awareness that is experiencing all of this - that remains. It's a hard concept to wrap your head around when you're in the thick of it, but trust me, it starts to sink in over time. It's like watching a wave crash on the shore. The wave disappears, but the ocean remains. You're the ocean, not just the wave.

Facing Your Shadow: The Death of Illusions

Kundalini doesn't just show you the light; it drags you through the muck. It forces you to confront every single shadow, every unexamined fear, every unresolved trauma you've been carrying around. And guess what? A lot of those fears are tied to death. Fear of abandonment, fear of not being good enough, fear of suffering, fear of the unknown - they all have a root in the ultimate fear of annihilation.

So, when you're feeling that intense anxiety, that sense of dread, it's not always just the energy itself. It's also the energy shining a spotlight on all the dark corners of your psyche that you've been avoiding. It's saying, "Hey, you've got to deal with this. You can't take this baggage with you." And dealing with it feels like a death. It feels like you're revisiting old wounds, dying those little deaths all over again. You're mourning the loss of illusions you held about yourself, about others, about the world. And that's painful. It's like a death of your innocence, a death of your naive beliefs.

I remember crying for days, sometimes weeks, over things I thought I'd long since processed. Old heartbreaks, childhood traumas, moments of shame - they all came flooding back, demanding to be seen and felt. It felt like I was dying a thousand tiny deaths, reliving every painful moment of my past. But here's the thing: you can't heal what you don't feel. And kundalini makes you feel everything. It's not gentle. It's a cosmic bulldozer. And it's clearing out all the debris that's been blocking your true light. So yeah, it feels like a death, because it's the death of all the emotional garbage you've been carrying around. And that's a good thing, even if it doesn't feel like it right now.

This process of facing your shadow is crucial. You can't bypass it. You can't meditate it away. You have to walk through it. And as you do, you'll find that the things you feared most about yourself, about life, about death - they start to lose their power. You realize that you're stronger than you ever imagined. You realize that you can survive these emotional deaths, and emerge on the other side, not just intact, but stronger, wiser, and more compassionate. It's a brutal initiation, but it's one that ultimately liberates you from the shackles of fear.

Living with a New Relationship to Mortality

After the initial storm, after the ego has taken its beating, and after you've faced some of your deepest fears, something shifts. Your relationship with death changes. It doesn't become something you seek out, but it also stops being the ultimate boogeyman. You start to see it for what it is: a natural part of the cycle of existence. It's not an end; it's a transition. It's not an annihilation; it's a transformation.

This new understanding doesn't mean you're suddenly fearless. You'll still have moments of human fear, because you're still in a human body. But the deep, existential terror that gripped you before - that starts to fade. You begin to live more fully, paradoxically, when you're less afraid of dying. You're not holding back, you're not playing small, because you realize that this life, this moment, is precious. And it's not going to last forever. So why waste it being afraid?

I've seen this in myself and in others who've gone through this. There's a newfound appreciation for the simple things. The warmth of the sun, the taste of good food, the laughter of a friend. These aren't just fleeting pleasures; they're moments of connection, moments of life, that you savor with a depth you couldn't access before. You're more present, more alive, because you've touched the edge of what it means to not be. Know what I mean? It puts everything into sharp relief.

Acceptance, Not Denial

It's not about denying death; it's about accepting it. It's about understanding its place in the grand scheme of things. Before, you might have pushed thoughts of death away, avoided conversations about it, tried to pretend it wasn't real. Now, you can look at it with a quiet understanding. It's not something to be feared, but something to be acknowledged. This acceptance brings a deep sense of peace. It's like finally making peace with an old enemy, only to realize it was never truly an enemy at all, just a misunderstood part of life.

This acceptance also extends to the "little deaths" that happen throughout life: the end of a relationship, the loss of a job, the changing of seasons. You see them all as part of the natural flow, the constant cycle of beginnings and endings. You become more resilient, more adaptable, because you've been through the ultimate dissolution of self and survived. You know, The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) talks a lot about this process of integration and how you learn to live with these new perceptions. It's not just about the big bang; it's about the aftermath and how you rebuild.

You'll find yourself less attached to outcomes, less attached to material possessions, less attached to the approval of others. Because you've been stripped bare, you realize what truly matters. And it's rarely what you thought it was before. This detachment isn't cold or uncaring; it's a deeper kind of freedom. It's the freedom that comes from knowing that even if you lose everything, the essence of who you are remains. Does that land? It's a tough pill to swallow when you're in the thick of it, but it's the truth.

Living with Purpose

When you've faced the abyss and come back, your priorities shift dramatically. Life becomes about something more than just personal gain or fleeting pleasures. There's often a deep desire to contribute, to serve, to make a difference. You realize how precious life is, and you want to live it with authenticity and meaning. The fear of death, which once paralyzed you, now becomes a motivator to live fully, to express your true self, to share your gifts with the world.

So yeah, kundalini can absolutely feel like you're dying. It can be the most terrifying experience of your life. But it's not a death of you. It's a death of the illusion of you. It's a death of fear, a death of separation, a death of all the things that kept you from truly living. And from that death, something incredible is born. A deeper understanding of life, a deep connection to everything, and a new, fearless relationship with mortality itself. You're going to get through this. It's dark now, I know, but there's light on the other side. Keep going. And if you're really struggling, don't try to go it alone. A Sourcebook for Helping People with Spiritual Problems by Emma Bragdon (paid link) might give you some ideas on where to find support. You're not crazy, and you're not alone.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
